title: NodeSplittingHard

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_12 -- "NodeSplittingHard".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_12
% title: NodeSplittingHard
\def\rowref{def\_3\_12}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_12}

\begin{Def}[Node-splitting hard intervention on CADMGs]
  \label{def:G_node-splitting_intervention}
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes.
    The \emph{single-world intervention graph (SWIG)} w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: 
    \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]
    %\[ G_{\swig(W)} := G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    %\[G((V\sm W) \dcup W^o|\doit(W^i \dcup J)):=G':=(J',V',E',L'),\]
    constructed as follows.
    We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$:
    \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC.  \]
    Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$.
  However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put:
  \[ v^o:=v^i:=v.  \]
  We then define:
  \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
      \item $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
      \item $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^o$.
      \item $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E  \rC$,
      \item $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.
  \end{enumerate}
  where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$,
  and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CADMG and $W \ins V$ a subset of the output nodes. The single-world intervention graph (SWIG) w.r.t.\ $W$ of $G$ is the CADMG: \[ G_{\swig(W)} :=\lp J_{\swig(W)}, V_{\swig(W)}, E_{\swig(W)},L_{\swig(W)} \rp,\]

constructed as follows. We first make two disjont copies of the nodes in $W$: \[ W^o:=\lC w^o\st w \in W \rC, \qquad W^i:=\lC w^i \st w \in W \rC. \] Note that we consider $w^o \neq w^i$ for $w \in W$. However, for brevity, for $v \in J \cup V \sm W$ we put: \[ v^o:=v^i:=v. \] We then define:

  1. $J_{\swig(W)} := J \dcup W^i$,
  2. $V_{\swig(W)} := (V \sm W) \dcup W^o$, %which could be identified with $V$ again if we want to make the identification $W=W^o$.
  3. $E_{\swig(W)} := \lC v^i_1 \tuh v_2^o \st v_1 \tuh v_2 \in E \rC$,
  4. $L_{\swig(W)} :=\lC v_1^o \huh v_2^o \st v_1 \huh v_2 \in L \rC$.

where we turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$, and we turn all nodes of $W^o$ into output nodes, removing all edges out of $W^o$.

lean_code_with_comments:
/-- The *single-world intervention graph (SWIG)* of the CDMG `G`
w.r.t. a set `W ⊆ G.V` of output nodes: the new CDMG over the
carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` obtained by first node-splitting on `W` (yielding
`w^o = Sum.inl w` and `w^i = Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩`) and then
hard-intervening on the freshly introduced `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr`
copies. See `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` definition
`def:G_node-splitting_intervention` (def 3.12 of the LN).

By construction this is exactly
`(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)`;
the four `@[simp]` lemmas `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J`,
`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V`,
`mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E`,
`mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L` below characterise the four
components and are the gateway for every downstream SWIG rewrite. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) : CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
    (Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W))

/-- Short-form alias for `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`, exposing
the LN's `\swig` macro / Richardson--Robins SWIG terminology as
the dot-notation `G.swig W hW`. The alias is purely cosmetic:
its job is to let downstream Lean prose (claim_3_11 here, and the
counterfactual / identification / iSCM machinery of chapters
4 -- 16) read like the lecture notes' `G_{\swig(W)}` rather than
the eight-syllable long-form name.

Declared as a `noncomputable abbrev` (not `def`) so that the four
`@[simp]` lemmas attached to `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` fire
on `G.swig W hW` terms by reducibility; a plain `def` would have
forced every downstream caller either to `unfold swig` first or to
maintain a parallel `swig_*` simp-lemma family. -/
noncomputable abbrev swig (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW

/-- The *input* nodes of `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`
are `Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range Sum.inr`: the original inputs
(carried under the canonical `inl` embedding) plus the fresh
intervention-input labels `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr`. This matches
the LN's `J_{swig(W)} = J ⊔ W^i` (def 3.12 item i.) under the
identification `α ≅ inl '' α` of `NodeSplittingOn.lean`. By
definition of the composition together with the `@[simp]` lemmas
`hardInterventionOn_J` and `nodeSplittingOn_J`. -/
@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).J =
      Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W) := by
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).J = _
  rw [hardInterventionOn_J, nodeSplittingOn_J]

/-- The *output* nodes of `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`
are `Sum.inl '' G.V`: the LN's `(V ∖ W) ⊔ W^o` collapses to all of
`Sum.inl '' G.V` under our identification `w^o = Sum.inl w` (so
`W^o ⊆ Sum.inl '' G.V` since `W ⊆ G.V`, and `(V ∖ W) ∪ W = V` when
`W ⊆ V`). Matches def 3.12 item ii. Proven by reducing to
`(Sum.inl '' G.V ∪ Set.range Sum.inr) \ Set.range Sum.inr` via the
NS / HI projection simp lemmas, then noting `Sum.inl '' G.V` is
disjoint from `Set.range Sum.inr` by constructor mismatch. -/
@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).V = Sum.inl '' G.V := by
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).V = _
  rw [hardInterventionOn_V, nodeSplittingOn_V, Set.union_diff_right]
  ext x
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, _⟩
    exact h
  · intro h
    refine ⟨h, ?_⟩
    obtain ⟨v, _, rfl⟩ := h
    rintro ⟨w, hw⟩
    cases hw

/-- *Directed-edge* membership in `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
W hW`: a pair `p` is a directed edge of the SWIG iff it is the
relabeling `(split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` of some original edge
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`. Matches def 3.12 item iii.'s set-builder
`{v_1^i → v_2^o | v_1 → v_2 ∈ E}` (source dispatches on `v_1 ∈ W`
via `split1`; target is always the canonical `inl`). The post-NS
piece 2 (split edges `w^o → w^i`) of `nodeSplittingOn_E` is killed
by the HI because its target `Sum.inr w` lies in `W^i = Set.range
Sum.inr`; the post-NS piece 1's target `Sum.inl v₂` never does. -/
@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).E ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E ∧ p = (split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by
  change p ∈ ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).E ↔ _
  rw [mem_hardInterventionOn_E, mem_nodeSplittingOn_E]
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, hn⟩
    rcases h with ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩ | ⟨w, rfl⟩
    · exact ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩
    · exact absurd ⟨w, rfl⟩ hn
  · rintro ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩
    refine ⟨Or.inl ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩, ?_⟩
    rintro ⟨w, hw⟩
    cases hw

/-- *Bidirected-edge* membership in
`G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`: a pair `p` is a bidirected
edge of the SWIG iff it is the double-`inl` relabeling
`(Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` of some original `(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L`.
Matches def 3.12 item iv.'s `L_{swig(W)} = {v_1^o ↔ v_2^o | v_1 ↔
v_2 ∈ L}`. The HI on `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr` is a no-op on
`nodeSplittingOn_L` because both endpoints of every edge in the
post-NS `L` are of the form `Sum.inl _` (constructor mismatch with
`Sum.inr`). -/
@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).L ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L ∧ p = (Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by
  change p ∈ ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).L ↔ _
  rw [mem_hardInterventionOn_L, mem_nodeSplittingOn_L]
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, _, _⟩
    exact h
  · rintro ⟨v₁, v₂, hL, rfl⟩
    refine ⟨⟨v₁, v₂, hL, rfl⟩, ?_, ?_⟩
    · rintro ⟨w, hw⟩; cases hw
    · rintro ⟨w, hw⟩; cases hw
lean_code_without_comments:
noncomputable def nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) : CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
    (Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W))

noncomputable abbrev swig (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW

@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).J =
      Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W) := by
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).J = _
  rw [hardInterventionOn_J, nodeSplittingOn_J]

@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).V = Sum.inl '' G.V := by
  change ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).V = _
  rw [hardInterventionOn_V, nodeSplittingOn_V, Set.union_diff_right]
  ext x
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, _⟩
    exact h
  · intro h
    refine ⟨h, ?_⟩
    obtain ⟨v, _, rfl⟩ := h
    rintro ⟨w, hw⟩
    cases hw

@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).E ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E ∧ p = (split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by
  change p ∈ ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).E ↔ _
  rw [mem_hardInterventionOn_E, mem_nodeSplittingOn_E]
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, hn⟩
    rcases h with ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩ | ⟨w, rfl⟩
    · exact ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩
    · exact absurd ⟨w, rfl⟩ hn
  · rintro ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩
    refine ⟨Or.inl ⟨v₁, v₂, hE, rfl⟩, ?_⟩
    rintro ⟨w, hw⟩
    cases hw

@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).L ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L ∧ p = (Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by
  change p ∈ ((G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
      (Set.range Sum.inr)).L ↔ _
  rw [mem_hardInterventionOn_L, mem_nodeSplittingOn_L]
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨h, _, _⟩
    exact h
  · rintro ⟨v₁, v₂, hL, rfl⟩
    refine ⟨⟨v₁, v₂, hL, rfl⟩, ?_, ?_⟩
    · rintro ⟨w, hw⟩; cases hw
    · rintro ⟨w, hw⟩; cases hw
lean_explanation:
The Lean definition takes three arguments: a CDMG `G : CDMG α` over an ambient vertex type `α : Type*`, a subset `W : Set α` of vertices to split off as intervention targets, and a proof `hW : W ⊆ G.V` that those vertices are output nodes (the LN's "$W \subseteq V$"). It produces a fresh `CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W)` over a strictly larger carrier. The dot-projection `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW` is the Lean spelling of the LN's subscript $G_{\swig(W)}$.

Rather than rebuild the four LN bullets field-by-field, the definition is the literal *composition* of the two sister operators introduced in def 3.10 and def 3.11: first node-split on $W$ (yielding the new carrier $\alpha \oplus W$, with $w^o = $ `Sum.inl w` and $w^i = $ `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩`), then hard-intervene on the freshly-introduced 1-copies. In Lean: `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W))`. The Mathlib idiom worth flagging is `Set.range f`, the image of all of `f`'s domain — here, the set of all `Sum.inr w` for $w \in W$, i.e. exactly the LN's $W^i$. The subtype `↑W` is `{x : α // x ∈ W}` (a vertex paired with a proof it lies in $W$), and `α ⊕ ↑W` is Mathlib's disjoint-sum type whose two constructors `Sum.inl` and `Sum.inr` capture "$w^o \neq w^i$" by *type* rather than by hypothesis.

A `noncomputable abbrev swig` then exposes the LN's `\swig` macro / Richardson–Robins SWIG terminology as the dot-notation `G.swig W hW`. Because it is an `abbrev` (not a `def`), it reduces by definitional unfolding, so any `@[simp]` lemma stated for `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` fires equally on `G.swig W hW` terms.

The four `@[simp]` characterization theorems at the bottom of the file recover the LN's four bullets in LN-faithful set-builder form. `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J` shows that the inputs are `Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range Sum.inr` — the LN's $J \sqcup W^i$ under the identification $\alpha \cong $ `Sum.inl '' α`. `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V` shows that the outputs collapse to `Sum.inl '' G.V` — the LN's $(V \setminus W) \sqcup W^o$ under $w^o = $ `Sum.inl w` (every $w \in W$ lies in $V$ by `hW`, so $W^o \subseteq $ `Sum.inl '' G.V`). `mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E` characterises a directed edge as `(split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` for some original $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ — the LN's $v_1^i \to v_2^o$ rule, where the helper `split1` realises $v \mapsto v^i$ (returning `Sum.inr ⟨v, _⟩` if `v ∈ W` and `Sum.inl v` otherwise). `mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L` characterises a bidirected edge as `(Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` for some original $(v_1, v_2) \in G.L$ — the LN's $v_1^o \leftrightarrow v_2^o$ rule. Each is proved in three to ten lines by `change` plus `rw` through the underlying NS / HI simp lemmas, together with the constructor-mismatch fact that no element of `Sum.inl '' _` lies in `Set.range Sum.inr`.
design_choices:
**Composition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)` rather than a fresh `CDMG` built field-by-field.** The four LN bullets line up *literally* with the two sister operators when one writes out the composition. Item i.) $J_{\swig(W)} = J \sqcup W^i$ falls out of `nodeSplittingOn_J` (which lifts $J$ via `Sum.inl`) post-adjoined with the intervention target `Set.range Sum.inr` $= W^i$. Item ii.) $V_{\swig(W)} = (V \setminus W) \sqcup W^o$ is what survives once the fresh copies are removed by the HI's set-difference on the outputs. Item iii.) $E_{\swig(W)} = \{v_1^i \to v_2^o\}$ is the post-NS piece-1 of the edges (source dispatched via `split1`, target via `Sum.inl`) — piece 2 (the fresh split edges $w^o \to w^i$) has target in `Set.range Sum.inr` and is killed by the HI. Item iv.) $L_{\swig(W)} = \{v_1^o \leftrightarrow v_2^o\}$ inherits the post-NS bidirected edges unchanged because both endpoints are of the form `Sum.inl _` and so cannot lie in `Set.range Sum.inr`. Defining the SWIG as this composition reuses every structural obligation already discharged by the two building blocks (`disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`); an independent field-by-field rebuild would re-prove roughly fifty lines of structural lemmas, and — worse — every downstream proof composing the SWIG with another `nodeSplittingOn` or `hardInterventionOn` would have to bridge a parallel API.

**Intervention target is `Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W)`, the post-split image of $W^i$.** In the $\alpha \oplus W$ carrier produced by `nodeSplittingOn`, the fresh intervention-input copies $W^i$ are precisely `Sum.inr`'s image of $\uparrow W$. Hard-intervening on this set is therefore the literal LN sentence at the bottom of def 3.12: *"turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$"*. This asymmetry — intervening on the *fresh* copies $W^i$ (lifted through `Sum.inr`) rather than the *canonical* copies $W^o$ (lifted through `Sum.inl`) — is what gives the SWIG its "single-world" character: the original outputs are preserved as observables while only the fresh $W^i$ carries the intervention.

**Short-form alias `swig` as a `noncomputable abbrev`, not a `def`.** The eight-syllable long-form `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` is descriptive but unwieldy in downstream proofs; `G.swig W hW` reads like the LN's $G_{\swig(W)}$ and matches the Richardson–Robins SWIG terminology used throughout the counterfactuals literature. Declared as an `abbrev`, it reduces by definitional unfolding, so the four `@[simp]` lemmas attached to `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` fire on `G.swig W hW` terms by reducibility. A plain `def` would have forced every downstream caller either to `unfold swig` first or to maintain a parallel `swig_*` simp-lemma family.

**Carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` and the `Sum.inl` / `Sum.inr` convention are inherited, not redecided.** No fresh convention is introduced at this row: `Sum.inl w = w^o` (canonical observation copy, identified with the original $w$) and `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ = w^i` (fresh intervention-input label) are fixed by the underlying node-splitting construction, where the longer justification lives — the LN's own hints "$W = W^o$" in def 3.11 and def 3.12, plus the Richardson–Robins SWIG convention $X = X^o$. The convention is worth flagging at this row because the four `@[simp]` lemmas speak in those terms (`Sum.inl '' G.J` in the input lemma, `Sum.inl '' G.V` in the output lemma, `split1 W v₁` and `Sum.inl v₂` in the directed-edge lemma, double-`Sum.inl` in the bidirected-edge lemma); a reader who skips the node-splitting source file would otherwise be surprised by constructor names appearing inside LN-faithful set-builder forms.
